title: oidcProvider({ requirePKCE: false }) — OIDC provider issues authorization codes without PKCE, enabling code-interception attacks

params.passDetail: requirePKCE is true (the default), so the OIDC provider rejects any authorization request lacking a code_challenge with 'pkce is required'. This is what OAuth 2.1 mandates for all authorization-code flows.
params.failDetail: requirePKCE: false disables PKCE enforcement — the provider will happily issue authorization codes to clients that send no code_challenge. An attacker who intercepts an authorization code (via a malicious app registered on a redirect scheme, referer leakage, or a compromised network) can redeem it directly, because there is no code_verifier binding the code to the original client. OAuth 2.1 REQUIRES PKCE. Fix: remove this option or set requirePKCE: true.
params.absentCallDetail: No oidcProvider() call in this scope; rule N/A here.
params.absentArgDetail: oidcProvider() is called but requirePKCE is not set; the default (true) applies and PKCE is enforced.

provenance.note: requirePKCE defaults to true, but the option exists specifically to turn it off, and the type doc even says 'According to OAuth2.1 spec this should be required. But ... if you want to disable this you can use this option.' AI agents copy-pasting to 'fix' a client that doesn't send PKCE will flip this to false and silently downgrade the whole provider to a code-interception-vulnerable flow. The enforcement in authorize.ts is gated entirely on `options.requirePKCE`.
